title: Security+ SY0-701 — Section 14: Authentication & Secure Protocols
description: Jason Dion Security+ SY0-701 — Section 14: Authentication Methods and Secure Network Protocols. Domain: General Security Concepts (1.0) & Security Architecture (3.0).

questions.question: What does AAA stand for in network security?
answers: Authentication, Authorization, Accounting
Access, Audit, Administration
Authentication, Auditing, Access Control
Authorization, Accountability, Anonymization
questions.explanation: AAA is a framework: Authentication (who are you?), Authorization (what can you do?), and Accounting (what did you do?). RADIUS and TACACS+ are common AAA protocols.

questions.question: What is a key difference between RADIUS and TACACS+?
answers: RADIUS combines authentication/authorization; TACACS+ separates them and encrypts the entire packet
RADIUS uses TCP; TACACS+ uses UDP
RADIUS is for VPNs; TACACS+ is for wireless
They are identical protocols
questions.explanation: TACACS+ (Cisco) separates authentication, authorization, and accounting and encrypts the entire payload over TCP. RADIUS combines authentication/authorization, only encrypts the password, and uses UDP.

questions.question: What is Kerberos and what problem does it solve?
answers: A ticket-based authentication protocol that avoids sending passwords over the network
An encryption algorithm for Windows Active Directory
A VPN protocol for secure remote access
A hash function for password storage
questions.explanation: Kerberos uses tickets issued by a KDC (Key Distribution Center) to authenticate users. Passwords are never transmitted over the network — only encrypted tickets, reducing interception risk.

questions.question: What authentication factor does a smart card represent?
answers: Something you have — a physical token storing credentials or certificates
Something you know — it stores a PIN
Something you are — it uses biometrics
Somewhere you are — it uses GPS location
questions.explanation: A smart card is a 'something you have' factor. It stores certificates or credentials on a chip and requires physical possession plus typically a PIN for MFA.

questions.question: How does TOTP (Time-based One-Time Password) work?
answers: Generates a short-lived OTP based on current time and a shared secret
Sends a password via SMS that expires after use
Creates a permanent password tied to a timestamp
Uses a hardware token that plugs into USB
questions.explanation: TOTP (RFC 6238) generates a new OTP every 30 seconds by combining a shared secret with the current time. Used by authenticator apps like Google Authenticator and Authy.

questions.question: What is FIDO2 and why is it considered more secure than passwords?
answers: A passwordless authentication standard using public key cryptography and hardware security keys
A password manager standard
A biometric authentication protocol
A replacement for RADIUS in enterprise environments
questions.explanation: FIDO2 (WebAuthn + CTAP) enables passwordless authentication using hardware keys (YubiKey) or device biometrics. The private key never leaves the device, making phishing and replay attacks impossible.

questions.question: Why should SSH be used instead of Telnet?
answers: SSH encrypts all traffic; Telnet transmits credentials and data in plaintext
SSH is faster than Telnet
Telnet requires more bandwidth
SSH works on more operating systems
questions.explanation: Telnet transmits everything including usernames and passwords in cleartext, easily intercepted by sniffers. SSH encrypts the entire session, protecting credentials and commands.

questions.question: What makes SFTP more secure than FTP?
answers: SFTP runs over SSH, encrypting files and credentials in transit
SFTP uses a different port that is harder to block
SFTP compresses files to reduce interception risk
SFTP requires two-factor authentication
questions.explanation: FTP transmits data and credentials in plaintext over separate control/data channels. SFTP tunnels file transfers through SSH, providing full encryption of both credentials and data.

questions.question: What port does HTTPS use and what does it protect?
answers: Port 443 — encrypts HTTP traffic between browser and web server using TLS
Port 80 — encrypts HTTP headers only
Port 8080 — provides authenticated web access
Port 22 — tunnels web traffic over SSH
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses port 443 and wraps HTTP in TLS, providing confidentiality (encrypted traffic), integrity (tamper detection), and authentication (server certificate verification).

questions.question: Why should SNMPv3 be used instead of SNMPv1/v2?
answers: SNMPv3 adds authentication and encryption; v1/v2 use community strings sent in cleartext
SNMPv3 supports more devices
SNMPv3 is faster for network monitoring
SNMPv1 and v2 are deprecated by IETF
questions.explanation: SNMPv1/v2 use community strings (essentially passwords) sent in plaintext — trivially sniffed. SNMPv3 adds authentication (HMAC) and optional encryption (DES/AES) for secure network management.

questions.question: What does DNSSEC protect against?
answers: DNS cache poisoning by digitally signing DNS records to verify authenticity
DNS traffic encryption between clients and servers
Unauthorized changes to DNS zone files
DDoS attacks against DNS servers
questions.explanation: DNSSEC adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records, allowing resolvers to verify that responses are authentic and haven't been tampered with — preventing cache poisoning attacks.

questions.question: What two protocols does IPSec use and what does each provide?
answers: AH (Authentication Header) for integrity; ESP (Encapsulating Security Payload) for encryption and integrity
IKE for key exchange; SSL for encryption
TLS for authentication; HMAC for integrity
L2TP for tunneling; AES for encryption
questions.explanation: IPSec uses AH to authenticate packet headers (no encryption), and ESP to encrypt and authenticate the payload. ESP is more commonly used as it provides both confidentiality and integrity.

questions.question: Why is CHAP more secure than PAP for authentication?
answers: CHAP uses a challenge-response mechanism and never transmits the password
CHAP uses longer passwords
CHAP encrypts the password before sending it
CHAP requires certificates
questions.explanation: PAP sends passwords in cleartext. CHAP uses a three-way challenge-response handshake where the password is never sent — only a hash of the challenge + password.

questions.question: What is S/MIME used for?
answers: Encrypting and digitally signing email messages
Securing MIME multimedia attachments in web browsers
Encrypting HTTP form submissions
Managing email server certificates
questions.explanation: S/MIME (Secure/Multipurpose Internet Mail Extensions) provides end-to-end encryption and digital signing for email, ensuring confidentiality and non-repudiation of email communications.

questions.question: What is adaptive/context-aware authentication?
answers: Adjusting authentication requirements based on risk signals like location, device, and behavior
Using different passwords for different applications
Requiring stronger passwords for admin accounts
Automatically resetting passwords based on time
questions.explanation: Adaptive authentication evaluates contextual risk factors (unusual location, new device, anomalous behavior) and dynamically adjusts authentication requirements — e.g., requiring MFA for high-risk logins.
